candidates.comparison: text prompt vs prompt_embeds representation with image_url, greedy decoding on same server
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on LLM-generated text, no tolerance
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- although embedding lookup is deterministic, async requests to a live server may be batched differently across the two calls, and Not Strong #9 applies
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #9: prompt text vs prompt_embeds equality when the only oracle is final generated text

candidates.comparison: text prompt vs prompt_embeds representation with image_embeds, greedy decoding on same server
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on LLM-generated text, no tolerance
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- async requests may land in different batch compositions; embedding path differences could emerge under different batch geometry
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #9: prompt text vs prompt_embeds equality when the only oracle is final generated text

candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on LLM-generated text, no tolerance
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- async requests may land in different batch compositions; embedding path differences could emerge under different batch geometry
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #9: prompt text vs prompt_embeds equality when the only oracle is final generated text

candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #9: prompt text vs prompt_embeds equality when the only oracle is final generated text

candidates.comparison: prompt_embeds item from mixed batch (text+embeds) vs prompt_embeds-only request
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on greedy-decoded LLM text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- the mixed batch has a different batch composition (text+embeds) vs the embeds-only request; without batch invariant mode, padding and batch geometry differences can cause numeric divergence
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #6: batch position invariance is not guaranteed without BI mode; Phase 1 incorrectly marked batch_invariant_enabled=true but VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is not set anywhere in this test or its conftest chain

candidates.comparison: prompt_embeds item from mixed batch (text+embeds) vs prompt_embeds-only request, with LoRA adapter
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on greedy-decoded LLM text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- mixed batch vs standalone request changes batch composition; LoRA adds further sensitivity to numeric path changes
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #6: batch position invariance not guaranteed without BI mode; Phase 1 incorrectly marked batch_invariant_enabled=true but VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT is not set

candidates.review: RECLASSIFY -- Phase 1 missed Strong Contract #7: duplicate identical requests in the same batch with the same sampling settings. The test sends ["Hello, my name is"] * 2 in a single batch with temperature=0.0. Both prompts are identical, in the same batch, with the same greedy sampling params. The batch geometry is identical for both items (same length, same padding). This is not a BS=1 vs BS=N comparison; it is two copies of the same prompt in the same batch.
candidates.comparison: two copies of same prompt in same batch, greedy decoding
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on greedy-decoded text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: disagree -- both items are identical prompts in the same batch; they see the same padding, same kernel invocations, same batch geometry. No realistic FP change would cause one copy to differ from the other within the same batch execution.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: disagree -- Strong Contract #7: duplicate identical requests in the same batch with the same sampling settings. Phase 1 incorrectly cited batch position invariance (Not Strong #6) but this is same-prompt-in-batch, not different-batch-size comparison.
candidates.code_snippet: assert batch.choices[0].text == batch.choices[1].text, "two copies of the same prompt should be the same"

candidates.review: RECLASSIFY -- Phase 1 missed Strong Contract #7: duplicate identical requests in the same batch. The test sends [prompt] * 2 with beam search n=2, then compares choices[0].text == choices[2].text (same beam rank across the two identical prompts). Both prompts are identical, in the same batch, with the same beam search settings.
candidates.comparison: same beam rank across two copies of identical prompt in same batch, beam search
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on beam-search-decoded text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: disagree -- both prompts are identical in the same batch with the same beam search config; they undergo the same kernel calls with the same batch geometry. No realistic FP change distinguishes one copy from the other within a single batch.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: disagree -- Strong Contract #7: duplicate identical requests in the same batch with the same sampling settings. Beam search does not change this contract; both copies are symmetric within the batch.
candidates.code_snippet: assert batch.choices[0].text == batch.choices[2].text, "two copies of the same prompt should be the same"

candidates.review: RECLASSIFY -- Phase 1 missed Strong Contract #7: duplicate identical requests in the same batch. The test sends [prompt] * 2 in a streaming batch, then compares texts[0] == texts[1]. Both prompts are identical, same batch, same greedy params. Streaming is just the transport layer (Strong Contract #6); the underlying computation is the same duplicate-in-batch pattern.
candidates.comparison: two copies of same prompt in same streaming batch, greedy decoding
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact string equality on streamed LLM text
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: disagree -- identical prompts in the same batch with identical padding see the same compute path. No FP change can distinguish between them.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: disagree -- Strong Contract #7: duplicate identical requests in the same batch with the same sampling settings. Streaming transport does not change the computation contract.

candidates.comparison: LLM-generated text checked for factual substring '12,742' (Earth diameter)
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- checks for a specific formatted substring in LLM-generated text; the model could express the number as '12742', '12,742 km', '~12,742', or use the equatorial diameter '12,756' instead
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- the prompt is batched with a structured output request, changing batch geometry. With seed=42 and temperature=0.0 for the non-structured request, greedy decoding is used, but the exact phrasing including comma-formatting is not guaranteed across FP changes
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #6: the non-structured request is batched with a structured request, creating a mixed batch geometry. No contract guarantees the exact substring format.

candidates.comparison: beam search output checked for semantic term presence (e.g., 'boardwalk' + 'wooden' for boardwalk image)
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- checks for specific semantic terms in LLM output; the model could describe the scene using different vocabulary
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- max_completion_tokens=10 is very tight; with only 10 tokens and beam search, FP numeric changes could shift beam rankings enough that different terms are chosen, especially since all term groups must match in both beam outputs
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- no contract requires specific descriptive terms to appear in model output; the terms are empirically chosen based on current model behavior

candidates.comparison: TP=1 vs TP=2 output for quantized (AWQ) LoRA model, greedy decoding
candidates.oracle: exact text list equality (assert output_tp1 == output_tp2)
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact text equality across TP configurations
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- different TP sizes use different all-reduce patterns, shard sizes, and potentially different GEMM algorithms, all of which are sensitive to FP numeric changes
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #4: tensor parallel exact generated output equality is not a strong contract

candidates.comparison: exact text/token equality via check_outputs_equal across different scheduling configs (async vs sync, preemption, executors, chunked prefill). Logprobs compared with rel_tol=1e-3, abs_tol=1e-6.
candidates.oracle: exact text+token equality (check_outputs_equal) plus logprob matching (_all_logprobs_match with tolerance)
candidates.c1_weak_oracle: agree -- exact text/token equality across scheduling configs; even with FP32 precision and greedy decoding, the text oracle is exact equality with no tolerance on the text/token output
candidates.c2_realistic_breakage: agree -- different scheduling configs (preemption=True vs False, chunk_prefill=True vs False, executor=mp vs uni) create different batch compositions and execution orders. Without VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT, preemption forces recomputation with different batch geometry. PyTorch changes to matmul algorithms or kernel selection could cause divergence.
candidates.c3_no_strong_contract: agree -- Not Strong #6: different scheduling/preemption configs change batch composition without batch invariant mode. The test uses VLLM_FLOAT32_MATMUL_PRECISION=highest and FLEX_ATTENTION to reduce variance, but does not enable VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT, so batch geometry differences can still cause divergence.
